---
title: "LRPITT19: Ability-adjusted modified-ITT offer effect on not-taught receptive vocabulary (UR)"

format:
  html:
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-links:
      - repo
---

{{< include _partials/_header.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_setup.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_gate_badge.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_key_findings.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_reading_guide.qmd >}}

## Overview

LRPITT19 is the **general-ability-adjusted available-case modified-ITT** analysis for not-taught receptive vocabulary, block 1 (b1rent) (`b1rent`)—
the ability-robustness companion to LRPITT03, part of the LRPITT17-24 ability-adjusted
family. It uses the uniform LRPITT spec plus a linear adjustment for **baseline
nonverbal cognitive ability** - block design (`blocks`), recorded at t1 only. The
estimand is the randomised offer effect on $UR_{\text{post}}$ in the outcome-available fitted sample over the RCT phase, conditional on ignorable exclusion and missingness—not a full-population effect for all 57 randomised children.

::: {.callout-note title="Why adjust for ability, and what it does (and does not) buy"}

Block design is a child trait measured **before** randomisation, so it cannot cause assignment. This adjustment is a **precision / chance-imbalance robustness check**, not confounding control. Randomisation balances it at assignment in expectation, although observed-data selection can alter balance in a fitted subset. The immediate-intervention arm started about **0.27 SD higher** in block design, and block design is prognostic of the outcomes, so adjustment asks whether the offer contrast survives that chance head-start.

Age is held in (`use_age_linear`), so the block-design term reads as ability **for age**—the general latent ability construct. Block design is observed for all 54 children in the repository, so it causes no additional exclusion beyond the outcome-specific analytic requirement: LRPITT19 versus LRPITT03 is a same-outcome-available-sample adjusted-versus-unadjusted contrast. It does not restore the three randomised children absent from the repository or any missing outcome.

:::

::: {.callout-note title="Identification and sign convention"}

Under the locked causal DAG, $G$ is a randomised root, so randomisation identifies the offer contrast $\tau$ within the outcome-available fitted sample without adjustment. Generalising beyond that sample requires ignorable exclusion and missingness. The own baseline, linear age, and the
block-design adjuster are **precision terms only** - they sharpen $\tau$ but are not
what licenses the causal interpretation. **Sign convention:** $G = 1$ is the
intervention arm, so a **positive** $\tau$ means the intervention raises the outcome.

:::

## Model

$$
\eta_i = \alpha + \tau\, G_i
       + \gamma_{\text{own}}\, \text{logit}_*(UR_{\mathrm{pre},i}, N)
       + \gamma_A\, A_{\text{std},i}
       + \gamma_{\text{blocks}}\, z(\text{blocks}_i),
$$

with Beta-Binomial likelihood $UR_{\text{post},i} \sim \text{BetaBinomial}(N, \mu_i\kappa, (1-\mu_i)\kappa)$,
$\mu_i = \sigma(\eta_i)$, $N = 12$. Priors follow the shared template in `priors.py`.
The block-design adjuster enters as a linear $\gamma_{\text{blocks}}$ term (the same
weak $\text{Normal}(0, 0.3)$ prior as cross-baseline couplings); HSGPs are off by
default.

![Model diagram](model_graph.png){#fig-model .lightbox fig-align="left"}

{{< include _partials/_results_itt.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_priors.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_prior_predictive.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_technical.qmd >}}
{{< include _partials/_footer.qmd >}}
